rules.foodCategory: Seasonings and condiments, snack foods (as part of food or feed commodity)
rules.limit: Verification required against Codex maximum levels (MLs)
rules.notes: Maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants are established by the Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) and are legally permitted concentrations. Codex works to keep these levels as low as possible based on sound scientific evidence.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.limit: Adherence to Code of Practice required

rules.notes: The EU Additives Database provides information on approved food additives and their conditions of use, based on Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.notes: Food additives must undergo safety assessment by SFA before use.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.notes: Food products containing non-permitted additives cannot be imported or sold. An application can be submitted to SFA for evaluation.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.notes: Food businesses must check the SFA's list or search tool for permitted additives and their corresponding limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed. Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Contaminants (incidental constituents) in food must be within SFA's regulatory limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Food products exceeding regulatory limits for contaminants or incidental constituents must not be imported or sold.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Specific limits for microbiological standards, heavy metals, mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, veterinary drug residues, and pesticide residues must be verified from SFA's documents.

rules.foodCategory: Meat and food derived from meat

rules.notes: Import or sale of meat and food derived from meat containing diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol residues is prohibited.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol... hexoestrol... dienoestrol...

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: SFA has established Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, effective from 1 May 2020. These MRLs are available on the SFA website.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.notes: All ingredients and additives in prepacked foods must be declared on the food label.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.notes: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food is not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.notes: This is a Guidance Level.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.notes: This is an Action Level. Specific action levels also exist for Brazil nuts, peanuts and peanut products, and pistachio nuts.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400

rules.parameter: Poisonous or deleterious substances (e.g., toxic elements, mycotoxins, persistent pesticides, radionuclides, other chemical contaminants)
rules.limit: Verification required against the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool, relevant guidance documents, and 21 CFR parts 109 and 509.
rules.notes: The tool provides tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Users are responsible for staying informed about changes.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA has published a variety of contaminant levels for poisonous or deleterious substances in human food in various guidance documents and regulations for industry. For ease of reference, the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Contaminant levels listed in the transparency tool include tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.limit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content.
rules.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling. Specific compliance requirements exist for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling... On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods... bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.notes: Advisory statements are not required by law and should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices have been incorporated. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact... advisory statements should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices and must be truthful and not misleading.

rules.foodCategory: Human food (establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold human food)
rules.notes: Establishments must implement written procedures to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117).
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA’s “Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food” rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements applicable to establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold human food. The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.foodCategory: All food and cosmetic products containing cochineal extract and carmine
rules.notes: Must be declared on the label as they are identified as allergenic substances.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine... have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.notes: The food source of a major food allergen must appear either in parentheses following the ingredient name or immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a "contains" statement. The specific type of tree nut, species of fish, and species of Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The allergen's food source must be declared at least once on the food label in one of two ways. The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient... — OR — Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement. The law also requires that the type of tree nut... and the species of fish... and Crustacean shellfish... to be declared.

rules.foodCategory: All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5
rules.notes: Required to be identified on labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.notes: Must be declared on food labels when present at or above 10 ppm total sulfites due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
